title: Name and territory of the Union
shortText: India, that is Bharat, shall be a Union of States
content: India, that is Bharat, shall be a Union of States. The States and the territories thereof shall be as specified in the First Schedule. The territory of India shall comprise the territories of the States, the Union territories specified in the First Schedule, and such other territories as may be acquired.
notes: Establishes the name and federal structure of India. 'Union of States' indicates indestructible union.

title: Admission or establishment of new States
shortText: Parliament may by law admit into the Union or establish new States
content: Parliament may by law admit into the Union, or establish, new States on such terms and conditions as it thinks fit.
notes: Enables Parliament to reorganize the territory of India.

title: Formation of new States and alteration of areas, boundaries or names
shortText: Parliament may form new States by separation, union, or alteration of boundaries
content: Parliament may by law form a new State by separation of territory from any State or by uniting two or more States or parts of States or by uniting any territory to a part of any State; increase or diminish the area of any State; alter the boundaries or name of any State.
notes: Bill requires President's recommendation. State legislature's opinion must be sought but is not binding.

title: Definition of State
shortText: The State includes the Government and Parliament of India and the Government and Legislature of each State
content: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires, 'the State' includes the Government and Parliament of India and the Government and the Legislature of each of the States and all local or other authorities within the territory of India or under the control of the Government of India.
notes: This definition is crucial for understanding against whom fundamental rights can be enforced. Includes statutory and non-statutory bodies exercising governmental functions.

title: Laws inconsistent with or in derogation of Fundamental Rights
shortText: Laws inconsistent with Fundamental Rights shall be void
content: All laws in force in the territory of India immediately before the commencement of this Constitution, in so far as they are inconsistent with the provisions of this Part, shall, to the extent of such inconsistency, be void. The State shall not make any law which takes away or abridges the rights conferred by this Part and any law made in contravention of this clause shall, to the extent of the contravention, be void.
notes: Provides for judicial review of legislation. 'Law' includes ordinances, orders, bye-laws, rules, regulations, notifications, customs, or usages having force of law.

title: Equality before law
shortText: The State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or the equal protection of the laws
content: The State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or the equal protection of the laws within the territory of India. Prohibition of discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex or place of birth. This embodies the concept of rule of law — that all persons are equally subject to the ordinary law of the land. It permits reasonable classification but forbids class legislation.
notes: Two concepts: (1) Equality before law (British — absence of special privilege), (2) Equal protection of laws (American — equal treatment in equal circumstances). Permits reasonable classification based on intelligible differentia with rational nexus to the object sought.

title: Prohibition of discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex or place of birth
shortText: The State shall not discriminate against any citizen on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth
content: The State shall not discriminate against any citizen on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth, or any of them. No citizen shall, on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth, or any of them, be subject to any disability, liability, restriction, or condition with regard to access to shops, public restaurants, hotels, and places of public entertainment, or use of wells, tanks, bathing ghats, roads, and places of public resort maintained wholly or partly out of State funds or dedicated to the use of the general public.
notes: Clause (3) allows State to make special provisions for women and children. Clause (4) allows special provisions for socially and educationally backward classes or for SC/STs. Clause (5) added by 93rd Amendment (2005) for educational institutions.

title: Equality of opportunity in matters of public employment
shortText: There shall be equality of opportunity for all citizens in matters relating to employment under the State
content: There shall be equality of opportunity for all citizens in matters relating to employment or appointment to any office under the State. No citizen shall, on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex, descent, place of birth, residence, or any of them, be ineligible for, or discriminated against in respect of, any employment or office under the State.
notes: Clause (4) permits reservation of appointments for backward classes not adequately represented. Clause (4A) added by 77th Amendment (1995) for reservation in promotion for SC/STs. Clause (4B) added by 81st Amendment (2000) for carry-forward of unfilled vacancies.

title: Abolition of Untouchability
shortText: Untouchability is abolished and its practice in any form is forbidden
content: Untouchability is abolished and its practice in any form is forbidden. The enforcement of any disability arising out of Untouchability shall be an offence punishable in accordance with law.
notes: Absolute right — no exceptions. Enforced through Protection of Civil Rights Act, 1955 and SC/ST (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989.

title: Abolition of titles
shortText: No title, not being a military or academic distinction, shall be conferred by the State
content: No title, not being a military or academic distinction, shall be conferred by the State. No citizen of India shall accept any title from any foreign State. No person who is not a citizen of India shall, while he holds any office of profit or trust under the State, accept without the consent of the President any title from any foreign State.
notes: National awards like Bharat Ratna and Padma awards are not 'titles' — Balaji Raghavan v. Union of India (1996).

title: Protection of certain rights regarding freedom of speech, etc.
shortText: All citizens shall have the right to freedom of speech and expression, to assemble peaceably, to form associations, to move freely, to reside and settle, and to practise any profession
content: All citizens shall have the right: (a) to freedom of speech and expression; (b) to assemble peaceably and without arms; (c) to form associations or unions or co-operative societies; (d) to move freely throughout the territory of India; (e) to reside and settle in any part of the territory of India; (g) to practise any profession, or to carry on any occupation, trade or business. These freedoms are subject to reasonable restrictions in the interests of sovereignty and integrity of India, security of the State, friendly relations with foreign States, public order, decency or morality, or in relation to contempt of court, defamation, or incitement to an offence.
notes: Six freedoms guaranteed. Right (f) to acquire property was deleted by 44th Amendment (1978). Reasonable restrictions must be imposed by law, not by executive order. Media freedom and right to information derived from Article 19(1)(a).

title: Protection in respect of conviction for offences
shortText: No person shall be convicted except for violation of law in force at time of offence; no double jeopardy; no self-incrimination
content: No person shall be convicted of any offence except for violation of a law in force at the time of the commission of the act charged as an offence, nor be subjected to a penalty greater than that which might have been inflicted under the law in force at the time of the commission of the offence. No person shall be prosecuted and punished for the same offence more than once. No person accused of any offence shall be compelled to be a witness against himself.
notes: Three protections: (1) Ex post facto law prohibition, (2) Double jeopardy, (3) Self-incrimination. Cannot be suspended even during Emergency under Article 359.

title: Protection of life and personal liberty
shortText: No person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law
content: No person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law. Through judicial interpretation, it includes: right to live with human dignity, right to livelihood, right to privacy (K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India, 2017), right to shelter, right to health, right to education, right to clean environment, right to speedy trial, right to legal aid, right against custodial violence, and right to travel abroad.
notes: Most litigated article. After Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978), 'procedure established by law' must be 'right, just, and fair'. Cannot be suspended during Emergency (44th Amendment).

title: Right to education
shortText: The State shall provide free and compulsory education to all children of the age of 6 to 14 years
content: The State shall provide free and compulsory education to all children of the age of six to fourteen years in such manner as the State may, by law, determine. Inserted by the 86th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2002 and implemented through the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009 (RTE Act).
notes: Inserted by 86th Amendment (2002). RTE Act 2009 provides for 25% reservation in private schools for economically weaker sections.

title: Protection against arrest and detention in certain cases
shortText: Every person arrested shall be informed of grounds of arrest and shall have right to consult a legal practitioner
content: No person who is arrested shall be detained in custody without being informed, as soon as may be, of the grounds for such arrest nor shall he be denied the right to consult, and to be defended by, a legal practitioner of his choice. Every person who is arrested and detained in custody shall be produced before the nearest magistrate within a period of twenty-four hours of such arrest excluding the time necessary for the journey from the place of arrest to the court of the magistrate.
notes: Clauses (1) and (2) do not apply to enemy aliens or persons detained under preventive detention laws. Clauses (4)-(7) deal with preventive detention — Advisory Board must report within 3 months.

title: Prohibition of traffic in human beings and forced labour
shortText: Traffic in human beings and begar and other similar forms of forced labour are prohibited
content: Traffic in human beings and begar and other similar forms of forced labour are prohibited and any contravention of this provision shall be an offence punishable in accordance with law.
notes: Enforced through Bonded Labour System (Abolition) Act, 1976 and Immoral Traffic (Prevention) Act, 1956. Available against private individuals, not just the State.

title: Prohibition of employment of children in factories, etc.
shortText: No child below the age of fourteen years shall be employed in any factory or mine or hazardous employment
content: No child below the age of fourteen years shall be employed to work in any factory or mine or engaged in any other hazardous employment.
notes: Supplemented by Child Labour (Prohibition and Regulation) Act, 1986 (amended 2016) and Juvenile Justice Act, 2015.

title: Freedom of conscience and free profession, practice and propagation of religion
shortText: All persons are equally entitled to freedom of conscience and the right to profess, practise and propagate religion
content: Subject to public order, morality and health, all persons are equally entitled to freedom of conscience and the right freely to profess, practise and propagate religion. Nothing in this article shall affect the operation of any existing law or prevent the State from making any law regulating or restricting any economic, financial, political or other secular activity associated with religious practice.
notes: Subject to public order, morality, and health. State can regulate secular activities associated with religion.

title: Freedom to manage religious affairs
shortText: Every religious denomination has the right to establish and maintain institutions for religious and charitable purposes
content: Subject to public order, morality and health, every religious denomination or any section thereof shall have the right: (a) to establish and maintain institutions for religious and charitable purposes; (b) to manage its own affairs in matters of religion; (c) to own and acquire movable and immovable property; and (d) to administer such property in accordance with law.
notes: Available to religious denominations, not individuals. State can regulate administration of religious property.

title: Freedom as to payment of taxes for promotion of any particular religion
shortText: No person shall be compelled to pay any taxes for the promotion of any particular religion
content: No person shall be compelled to pay any taxes, the proceeds of which are specifically appropriated in payment of expenses for the promotion or maintenance of any particular religion or religious denomination.
notes: Prohibits only taxes specifically earmarked for religious promotion. General taxes that may indirectly benefit religious institutions are not prohibited.

title: Freedom as to attendance at religious instruction in educational institutions
shortText: No religious instruction shall be provided in State-funded educational institutions
content: No religious instruction shall be provided in any educational institution wholly maintained out of State funds. No person attending any educational institution recognised by the State or receiving aid out of State funds shall be required to take part in any religious instruction or worship without consent.
notes: Minority educational institutions under Article 30 can provide religious instruction.

title: Protection of interests of minorities
shortText: Any section of citizens having a distinct language, script or culture shall have the right to conserve the same
content: Any section of the citizens residing in the territory of India or any part thereof having a distinct language, script or culture of its own shall have the right to conserve the same. No citizen shall be denied admission into any educational institution maintained by the State or receiving aid out of State funds on grounds only of religion, race, caste, language or any of them.
notes: Available to any section of citizens, not limited to minorities. Read with Article 30.

title: Right of minorities to establish and administer educational institutions
shortText: All minorities shall have the right to establish and administer educational institutions of their choice
content: All minorities, whether based on religion or language, shall have the right to establish and administer educational institutions of their choice. The State shall not, in granting aid to educational institutions, discriminate against any educational institution on the ground that it is under the management of a minority.
notes: Key cases: TMA Pai Foundation (2002), St. Stephen's College (1992). RTE Act 2009 exempts minority institutions from 25% reservation.

title: Remedies for enforcement of Fundamental Rights
shortText: The right to move the Supreme Court for enforcement of Fundamental Rights is guaranteed
content: The right to move the Supreme Court by appropriate proceedings for the enforcement of the rights conferred by this Part is guaranteed. The Supreme Court shall have power to issue directions or orders or writs, including writs in the nature of habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition, quo warranto, and certiorari, for the enforcement of any of the rights conferred by this Part.
notes: Called the 'heart and soul of the Constitution' by Dr. B.R. Ambedkar. Five writs: Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Certiorari, Quo Warranto. This right itself is a fundamental right.

title: Power of Parliament to modify rights for Armed Forces
shortText: Parliament may restrict fundamental rights for members of armed forces, police, and intelligence
content: Parliament may, by law, determine to what extent any of the rights conferred by this Part shall, in their application to the members of the Armed Forces, the Forces charged with the maintenance of public order, persons employed in intelligence or counter-intelligence, be restricted or abrogated so as to ensure the proper discharge of their duties and the maintenance of discipline among them.
notes: Only Parliament can modify, not State Legislature. Applied to Army Act, Air Force Act, Navy Act.

title: Legislation to give effect to this Part
shortText: Parliament shall have power to make laws for prescribing punishment for acts declared offences under this Part
content: Notwithstanding anything in this Constitution, Parliament shall have, and the Legislature of a State shall not have, power to make laws with respect to any of the matters which under clause (3) of Article 16, clause (3) of Article 32, Article 33 and Article 34 may be provided for by law made by Parliament.
notes: Centralises legislative power for certain fundamental rights matters to ensure uniformity.

title: Definition of State (Part IV)
shortText: In this Part, the State has the same meaning as in Article 12
content: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires, 'the State' has the same meaning as in Article 12.
notes: Directive Principles are not enforceable by courts (Article 37) but are fundamental in governance.

title: Application of the principles contained in this Part
shortText: DPSP shall not be enforceable by any court but are fundamental in governance
content: The provisions contained in this Part shall not be enforceable by any court, but the principles therein laid down are nevertheless fundamental in the governance of the country and it shall be the duty of the State to apply these principles in making laws.
notes: Not justiciable but supplement fundamental rights. Minerva Mills v. Union of India (1980) — balance between fundamental rights and DPSPs.

title: State to secure a social order for the promotion of welfare of the people
shortText: The State shall strive to promote the welfare of the people by securing social order with justice
content: The State shall strive to promote the welfare of the people by securing and protecting as effectively as it may a social order in which justice, social, economic and political, shall inform all the institutions of the national life. The State shall strive to minimise inequalities in income and eliminate inequalities in status, facilities and opportunities.
notes: Clause (2) added by 44th Amendment (1978). Broadest DPSP covering social, economic, and political justice.

title: Certain principles of policy to be followed by the State
shortText: The State shall direct its policy towards adequate livelihood, equal pay, prevention of concentration of wealth
content: The State shall direct its policy towards securing: (a) adequate means of livelihood for citizens equally; (b) distribution of material resources for common good; (c) prevention of concentration of wealth; (d) equal pay for equal work for men and women; (e) protection of health and strength of workers; (f) opportunities for children to develop in a healthy manner.
notes: Socialistic principles. Equal pay for equal work has been treated as enforceable through Article 14/16.

title: Equal justice and free legal aid
shortText: The State shall provide free legal aid to ensure justice is not denied by reason of economic disabilities
content: The State shall secure that the operation of the legal system promotes justice, on a basis of equal opportunity, and shall provide free legal aid to ensure that opportunities for securing justice are not denied to any citizen by reason of economic or other disabilities.
notes: Inserted by 42nd Amendment (1976). Implemented through Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987 and NALSA.

title: Organisation of village panchayats
shortText: The State shall organise village panchayats as units of self-government
content: The State shall take steps to organise village panchayats and endow them with such powers and authority as may be necessary to enable them to function as units of self-government.
notes: Implemented through 73rd Constitutional Amendment (1992) adding Part IX on Panchayats.

title: Right to work, to education and to public assistance
shortText: The State shall make provision for right to work, education, and public assistance
content: The State shall, within the limits of its economic capacity, make effective provision for securing the right to work, to education and to public assistance in cases of unemployment, old age, sickness and disablement.
notes: Right to education made fundamental under Article 21A. MGNREGA 2005 partially implements right to work.

title: Living wage for workers
shortText: The State shall endeavour to secure a living wage and decent standard of life for all workers
content: The State shall endeavour to secure to all workers a living wage, conditions of work ensuring a decent standard of life and full enjoyment of leisure and social and cultural opportunities, and to promote cottage industries in rural areas.
notes: Minimum Wages Act, 1948 partially implements this. 'Living wage' is higher than 'minimum wage'.

title: Uniform Civil Code for the citizens
shortText: The State shall endeavour to secure a Uniform Civil Code throughout the territory of India
content: The State shall endeavour to secure for the citizens a uniform civil code throughout the territory of India.
notes: Most debated DPSP. Not yet fully implemented. Shah Bano Case (1985) and Sarla Mudgal v. Union of India (1995). Goa has a uniform civil code inherited from Portuguese law.

title: Early childhood care and education for children below six years
shortText: The State shall provide early childhood care and education for children below six years
content: The State shall endeavour to provide early childhood care and education for all children until they complete the age of six years.
notes: Substituted by 86th Amendment (2002). ICDS scheme implements this.

title: Promotion of educational and economic interests of SC, ST and weaker sections
shortText: The State shall promote educational and economic interests of SC/ST and weaker sections
content: The State shall promote with special care the educational and economic interests of the weaker sections of the people, and in particular of the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes, and shall protect them from social injustice and all forms of exploitation.
notes: Implemented through reservation policies, SC/ST (Prevention of Atrocities) Act 1989, scholarship schemes.

title: Duty of the State to raise nutrition and standard of living
shortText: The State shall regard raising nutrition and standard of living as a primary duty
content: The State shall regard the raising of the level of nutrition and the standard of living of its people and the improvement of public health as among its primary duties and shall endeavour to bring about prohibition of intoxicating drinks and drugs injurious to health.
notes: Several states have enacted prohibition laws (Gujarat, Bihar). National Food Security Act, 2013 partially implements this.

title: Organisation of agriculture and animal husbandry
shortText: The State shall organise agriculture on modern lines and prohibit slaughter of cows
content: The State shall endeavour to organise agriculture and animal husbandry on modern and scientific lines and shall take steps for preserving and improving the breeds, and prohibiting the slaughter, of cows and calves and other milch and draught cattle.
notes: Several states have enacted cow slaughter prohibition laws. State of Gujarat v. Mirzapur Moti Kureshi (2005) upheld total ban.

title: Protection and improvement of environment and safeguarding of forests and wildlife
shortText: The State shall protect environment and safeguard forests and wildlife
content: The State shall endeavour to protect and improve the environment and to safeguard the forests and wild life of the country.
notes: Inserted by 42nd Amendment (1976). Read with Article 51A(g). Wildlife Protection Act (1972), Environment Protection Act (1986).

title: Separation of judiciary from executive
shortText: The State shall take steps to separate the judiciary from the executive
content: The State shall take steps to separate the judiciary from the executive in the public services of the State.
notes: CrPC 1973 separated judicial and executive magistrates. Independence of judiciary is part of basic structure.

title: Promotion of international peace and security
shortText: The State shall promote international peace, maintain just relations among nations
content: The State shall endeavour to: (a) promote international peace and security; (b) maintain just and honourable relations between nations; (c) foster respect for international law and treaty obligations; and (d) encourage settlement of international disputes by arbitration.
notes: Forms basis of India's foreign policy of non-alignment and peaceful co-existence.

title: Fundamental Duties
shortText: It shall be the duty of every citizen to abide by the Constitution, cherish its ideals, protect sovereignty, defend the country, and promote harmony
content: It shall be the duty of every citizen of India: (a) to abide by the Constitution and respect its ideals and institutions, the National Flag and the National Anthem; (b) to cherish and follow the noble ideals which inspired our national struggle for freedom; (c) to uphold and protect the sovereignty, unity and integrity of India; (d) to defend the country and render national service when called upon; (e) to promote harmony and the spirit of common brotherhood amongst all the people of India transcending religious, linguistic and regional diversities; to renounce practices derogatory to the dignity of women; (f) to value and preserve the rich heritage of our composite culture; (g) to protect and improve the natural environment including forests, lakes, rivers and wildlife; (h) to develop the scientific temper, humanism and the spirit of inquiry and reform; (i) to safeguard public property and to abjure violence; (j) to strive towards excellence in all spheres of individual and collective activity; (k) to provide opportunities for education to child between 6 and 14 years.
notes: Added by 42nd Amendment (1976) on Swaran Singh Committee recommendation. Duty (k) added by 86th Amendment (2002). 11 duties total. Not directly enforceable but courts consider them while interpreting laws.

title: Power of President to grant pardons
shortText: The President has power to grant pardons, reprieves, respites or remissions of punishment
content: The President shall have the power to grant pardons, reprieves, respites or remissions of punishment or to suspend, remit or commute the sentence of any person convicted of any offence: (a) in all cases where the punishment is by Court Martial; (b) in cases involving Union law; (c) in all cases where the sentence is death.
notes: Kehar Singh v. Union of India (1989) — power is very wide. Different from Governor's power under Article 161.

title: Power of President to promulgate Ordinances
shortText: The President may promulgate Ordinances during recess of Parliament if immediate action is necessary
content: If at any time, except when both Houses of Parliament are in session, the President is satisfied that circumstances exist which render it necessary for him to take immediate action, he may promulgate Ordinances having the same force as an Act of Parliament. Every such Ordinance shall cease to operate at the expiration of six weeks from the reassembly of Parliament.
notes: D.C. Wadhwa v. State of Bihar (1987) — repeated re-promulgation is unconstitutional. Krishna Kumar Singh v. State of Bihar (2017) — ordinance subject to judicial review.

title: Establishment and constitution of Supreme Court
shortText: There shall be a Supreme Court of India with Chief Justice and other Judges
content: There shall be a Supreme Court of India consisting of a Chief Justice of India and such number of other Judges as Parliament may prescribe. Judges are appointed by the President after consultation with judges of the Supreme Court and High Courts.
notes: Currently 34 judges including CJI. Three Judges Cases (1982, 1993, 1998) established collegium system. NJAC struck down in 2015.

title: Special leave to appeal by the Supreme Court
shortText: The Supreme Court may grant special leave to appeal from any judgment of any court or tribunal
content: Notwithstanding anything in this Chapter, the Supreme Court may, in its discretion, grant special leave to appeal from any judgment, decree, determination, sentence or order in any cause or matter passed or made by any court or tribunal in the territory of India.
notes: Widest appellate jurisdiction. SLP is discretionary. Most common method of approaching Supreme Court.

title: Law declared by Supreme Court to be binding on all courts
shortText: The law declared by the Supreme Court shall be binding on all courts in India
content: The law declared by the Supreme Court shall be binding on all courts within the territory of India.
notes: Establishes precedent system. Ratio decidendi is binding, obiter dicta is persuasive. SC can overrule its own decisions.

title: Enforcement of decrees and orders of Supreme Court
shortText: The Supreme Court may pass orders as necessary for doing complete justice
content: The Supreme Court in the exercise of its jurisdiction may pass such decree or make such order as is necessary for doing complete justice in any cause or matter pending before it, enforceable throughout India.
notes: Extraordinary power for 'complete justice'. Used in Bhopal Gas Tragedy case, environmental protection orders, and dissolution of marriage cases.

title: Power of Governor to grant pardons
shortText: The Governor has power to grant pardons for offences against State law
content: The Governor of a State shall have the power to grant pardons, reprieves, respites or remissions of punishment or to suspend, remit or commute the sentence of any person convicted of any offence against any law relating to a matter to which the executive power of the State extends.
notes: Unlike President, Governor cannot pardon death sentence or Court Martial sentences. Exercised on advice of State Council of Ministers.

title: Power of High Courts to issue writs
shortText: High Courts may issue writs for enforcement of fundamental rights and for any other purpose
content: Every High Court shall have power to issue to any person or authority directions, orders or writs, including habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition, quo warranto and certiorari, for the enforcement of any fundamental rights and for any other purpose.
notes: Wider than Article 32 — can be invoked for fundamental rights AND any other legal right. Subject to territorial jurisdiction.

title: Right to property
shortText: No person shall be deprived of his property save by authority of law
content: No person shall be deprived of his property save by authority of law.
notes: Downgraded from fundamental right to constitutional right by 44th Amendment (1978). State cannot take property without authority of law.

title: Proclamation of Emergency
shortText: The President may proclaim Emergency if security of India is threatened by war, external aggression, or armed rebellion
content: If the President is satisfied that a grave emergency exists whereby the security of India is threatened by war or external aggression or armed rebellion, he may by Proclamation declare Emergency. Must be approved by Parliament within 1 month by special majority.
notes: 44th Amendment changed 'internal disturbance' to 'armed rebellion'. National Emergency imposed 3 times: 1962, 1971, 1975. Articles 20 and 21 cannot be suspended during Emergency.

title: President's Rule — failure of constitutional machinery in States
shortText: The President may assume functions of State Government if constitutional machinery fails
content: If the President is satisfied that the government of a State cannot be carried on in accordance with the Constitution, he may by Proclamation assume all functions of the State Government and declare that powers of the State Legislature shall be exercisable by Parliament.
notes: Must be approved by Parliament within 2 months. Maximum 3 years. S.R. Bommai v. Union of India (1994) — landmark case limiting misuse. Imposed over 100 times since 1950.

title: Financial Emergency
shortText: The President may declare Financial Emergency if financial stability of India is threatened
content: If the President is satisfied that the financial stability or credit of India is threatened, he may by Proclamation declare Financial Emergency. During such emergency, the President may direct reduction of salaries of government servants including judges.
notes: Never been proclaimed in India's history. Must be approved by Parliament within 2 months.

title: Power of Parliament to amend the Constitution
shortText: Parliament may amend the Constitution by special majority procedure
content: Parliament may in exercise of its constituent power amend by way of addition, variation or repeal any provision of this Constitution. The Bill must be passed in each House by a majority of total membership and by not less than two-thirds of members present and voting.
notes: Three types: simple majority, special majority, special majority + state ratification. Basic structure cannot be amended — Kesavananda Bharati (1973). 105+ amendments as of 2023.
